summary: DeVault addresses the common problem of FLOSS maintainer burnout and outlines two responsible strategies for stepping away from a project. The 'fork it' approach involves adding an unmaintained notice to the README and directing users to fork the project, offering a quick exit. The 'hand-off' approach involves mentoring contributors into co-maintainer roles and gradually transferring responsibilities. He argues both are legitimate and that no maintainer should feel shame about stepping down. The post emphasizes that volunteer work should remain fulfilling, and that constructive transitions serve the community better than silent abandonment.
key_points: FLOSS maintenance is volunteer work and maintainers are free to stop at any time without guilt
The 'fork it' method is safer than handing control to strangers because it requires the community to actively acknowledge the transfer of power
The 'hand-off' method through mentoring contributors into co-maintainers is less disruptive and builds community resilience
Delegating responsibilities can restore a maintainer's enthusiasm by letting them focus on work they enjoy
Building co-maintainers is good practice even when not planning to leave, as it protects against 'author existence failure'
money_quotes: It's no secret that maintaining free and open source software is often a burdensome and thankless job.
FLOSS work is volunteer work, and you're free to stop volunteering at any time.

This 'fork it' approach is safer than giving control of your project to passerby, because it requires your users to acknowledge the transfer of power, instead of being surprised by a new maintainer in a trusted package.
Taking this approach can also help you find healthier ways to be involved in your own project.
It's important to always be happy in your work, and especially in your volunteer work. If it's not working, then change it.
There's no shame in any of these changes - you still deserve pride in your accomplishments, and seeking constructive solutions to burnout would do your community a great service.
key_insight: Abandoning a FLOSS project responsibly means either empowering the community to fork or mentoring contributors into maintainers — both are valid, and neither warrants shame.
